---
title: "What Happens If I Flip the Sign? An Exploration from First-principles of Split-complex Numbers"
subtitle: ""
categories: [math,  exploration,  2026]
date: "2026-04-08"
draft: true
execute:
  echo: false
---

<style>
.cell-output-display,
.cell,
div.sourceCode,
div.column {
  overflow: visible !important;
}
.viz-wrap {
  margin: 1.8em 0;
}
.dual-panel {
  display: flex;
  gap: 20px;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  justify-content: center;
}
.dual-panel > div {
  flex: 1 1 340px;
  max-width: 460px;
}
.viz-label {
  font-size: 0.85em;
  color: #6b7280;
  text-align: center;
  margin-top: 4px;
}
.foil-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr;
  gap: 6px;
  max-width: 360px;
  margin: 0 auto;
}
.foil-cell {
  text-align: center;
  padding: 8px;
  border-radius: 6px;
  font-family: 'KaTeX_Math', serif;
  font-size: 1.05em;
}
@media (max-width: 750px) {
  .dual-panel { flex-direction: column; align-items: center; }
  .dual-panel > div { max-width: 100%; }
}
</style>

```{ojs}
// ── Shared utility functions ──────────────────────────────────────────────
toAB = (u, v) => ({a: u + v, b: u - v})
toUV = (a, b) => ({u: (a + b) / 2, v: (a - b) / 2})
normSC = (u, v) => u * u - v * v
scMul = (x, y) => ({
  u: x.u * y.u + x.v * y.v,
  v: x.u * y.v + x.v * y.u
})

COL = ({
  ePlus: "#10b981",
  eMinus: "#8b5cf6",
  complex: "#3b82f6",
  hyper: "#ef4444",
  zd: "#9ca3af"
})

// Reusable: snap a value to one decimal place within bounds
snap = (val, lo, hi) => Math.max(lo, Math.min(hi, Math.round(val * 10) / 10))

// Reusable: extract pixel↔data scales from an Observable Plot
plotScales = (plot) => {
  const sx = plot.scale("x"), sy = plot.scale("y");
  return {
    xScale: d3.scaleLinear().domain(sx.domain).range(sx.range),
    yScale: d3.scaleLinear().domain(sy.domain).range(sy.range)
  };
}

// Reusable: attach drag to all circles in a plot, calling onDrag(u,v) and onEnd(u,v)
attachDrag = (plot, {onDrag, onEnd, domain: [lo, hi]}) => {
  const {xScale, yScale} = plotScales(plot);
  d3.select(plot).selectAll("circle")
    .style("cursor", "grab")
    .call(d3.drag()
      .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
      .on("drag", function(event) {
        const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
        const u = snap(xScale.invert(px), lo, hi);
        const v = snap(yScale.invert(py), lo, hi);
        d3.select(this).attr("cx", xScale(u)).attr("cy", yScale(v));
        if (onDrag) onDrag.call(this, u, v, plot);
      })
      .on("end", function(event) {
        d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
        const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
        const u = snap(xScale.invert(px), lo, hi);
        const v = snap(yScale.invert(py), lo, hi);
        if (onEnd) onEnd(u, v);
      })
    );
  return plot;
}
```

I have been brushing up on my abstract algebra lately. At the moment I am reading *Linear Algebra Done Right* and Aluffi’s *Algebra: Notes from the Underground*. To practice the ideas, I decided, at least for a while, not to do the book exercises. Instead, I wanted to experiment on my own—not to discover anything new, but to make the discovery myself, slowly enough to get to know the resulting structure from the inside.

I started with a very small question. We all learn early on that the complex numbers arise by adjoining a formal symbol $i$ with the rule

$$
i^2=-1.
$$

So I wondered what would happen if I made one tiny change to that story. What if I adjoined a new symbol $t$ with the rule

$$
t^2=1?
$$

At first this looked like a harmless variation. It turned out not to be harmless at all. That one sign change led me, step by step, into the split-complex numbers, then into orthogonal idempotents, then into function algebras on finite sets, and finally into the subtler infinite-dimensional story where indicator functions behave like multiplicative atoms and, under the right hypotheses, even become an honest orthonormal basis.

What follows is that path of discovery, cleaned up from my scratch work but still written in the order in which the structure gradually revealed itself.

## 1. Starting from first principles: adjoining $t$ with $t^2=1$

Take all formal expressions of the form

$$
x=u+vt,
$$

where $u,v
\in\mathbb R$, and define addition and multiplication exactly as one would expect, using distributivity together with the single rule $t^2=1$.

If

$$
x=u+vt,
\qquad
y=a+bt,
$$

then

$$
x+y=(u+a)+(v+b)t
$$

and

$$
xy=(u+vt)(a+bt)=ua+ubt+vat+vbt^2=(ua+vb)+(ub+va)t.
$$

So this gives a $2$-dimensional real algebra. It looks suspiciously like the complex numbers, except that the basic quadratic relation has the opposite sign. In $\mathbb C$ we impose $i^2=-1$; here we are imposing $t^2=1$.

That tiny formal change turns out to alter the structure completely.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: what does the algebra look like for different signs?** The rule $t^2 = c$ defines a whole family of algebras, parametrized by $c$. Drag the slider to watch the geometry morph. At $c=-1$ you get the familiar complex numbers (circle); at $c=0$, the degenerate dual numbers; at $c=1$, the split-complex numbers (hyperbola). The dashed lines are the **zero divisor set** $u^2 = cv^2$ — notice how they exist only for $c > 0$, and disappear entirely for $c < 0$. Drag the black point to read off the norm $N = u^2 - cv^2$.

```{ojs}
viewof cParam = Inputs.range([-1.5, 1.5], {
  value: -1, step: 0.01, width: 500,
  label: "c  (defining  t² = c)"
})
```

```{ojs}
mutable pt1_1 = ({u: 1.5, v: 0.5})
```

```{ojs}
viz1_1 = {
  const c = cParam;
  const D = 5;
  const sRange = Array.from({length: 401}, (_, i) => -3.5 + i * 0.0175);
  const thetaRange = Array.from({length: 401}, (_, i) => i * 2 * Math.PI / 400);

  // ── Background heatmap (norm = u² - cv²) ─────────────────────────────
  const step = 0.2;
  const heatData = [];
  for (let u = -D; u <= D; u += step)
    for (let v = -D; v <= D; v += step)
      heatData.push({u, v, norm: u * u - c * v * v});

  // ── Unit curve  u² − cv² = 1 ──────────────────────────────────────────
  let unitRight = [], unitLeft = [];
  if (c < -0.001) {
    const ac = -c;
    unitRight = thetaRange.map(th => ({u: Math.cos(th), v: Math.sin(th) / Math.sqrt(ac)}));
  } else if (c > 0.001) {
    unitRight = sRange.map(s => ({u:  Math.cosh(s), v: Math.sinh(s) / Math.sqrt(c)}));
    unitLeft  = sRange.map(s => ({u: -Math.cosh(s), v: Math.sinh(s) / Math.sqrt(c)}));
  }

  // ── Anti-unit curve  u² − cv² = −1 ────────────────────────────────────
  let antiTop = [], antiBot = [];
  if (c > 0.001) {
    antiTop = sRange.map(s => ({u: Math.sinh(s), v:  Math.cosh(s) / Math.sqrt(c)}));
    antiBot = sRange.map(s => ({u: Math.sinh(s), v: -Math.cosh(s) / Math.sqrt(c)}));
  }

  // ── Zero-divisor lines  u = ±√c · v ───────────────────────────────────
  const zdSlope = c > 0.001 ? Math.sqrt(c) : null;

  // ── Annotation ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  let anno = "";
  if (Math.abs(c - 1) < 0.06) anno = "Split-complex  (t² = 1)";
  else if (Math.abs(c) < 0.06) anno = "Dual numbers  (ε² = 0)";
  else if (Math.abs(c + 1) < 0.06) anno = "Complex  (i² = −1)";

  // ── Assemble marks ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const marks = [
    // heatmap
    Plot.rect(heatData, {
      x1: d => d.u - step / 2, x2: d => d.u + step / 2,
      y1: d => d.v - step / 2, y2: d => d.v + step / 2,
      fill: "norm", stroke: null, tip: false
    }),
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#bbb"}),
  ];

  // c ≈ 0 degenerate: two vertical lines u = ±1
  if (Math.abs(c) <= 0.05) {
    marks.push(Plot.ruleX([1, -1], {stroke: COL.complex, strokeWidth: 2.5}));
  }

  // unit curve
  if (unitRight.length)
    marks.push(Plot.line(unitRight, {x: "u", y: "v", stroke: COL.complex, strokeWidth: 2.5}));
  if (unitLeft.length)
    marks.push(Plot.line(unitLeft,  {x: "u", y: "v", stroke: COL.complex, strokeWidth: 2.5}));

  // anti-unit curve
  if (antiTop.length)
    marks.push(Plot.line(antiTop, {x: "u", y: "v", stroke: COL.hyper, strokeWidth: 2.5}));
  if (antiBot.length)
    marks.push(Plot.line(antiBot, {x: "u", y: "v", stroke: COL.hyper, strokeWidth: 2.5}));

  // zero-divisor lines
  if (zdSlope !== null) {
    marks.push(
      Plot.line([[-D, -D / zdSlope], [D, D / zdSlope]], {
        stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
      Plot.line([[-D, D / zdSlope], [D, -D / zdSlope]], {
        stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5})
    );
  } else if (Math.abs(c) < 0.06) {
    marks.push(Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}));
  }

  // annotation
  if (anno)
    marks.push(Plot.text([{x: 0, y: D - 0.4}], {
      x: "x", y: "y", text: [anno], fontSize: 13, fontWeight: "bold", fill: "#374151"
    }));

  const plot = Plot.plot({
    width: 560, height: 560,
    grid: true,
    aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, D], label: "u  (real part)"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "v  (t-part)"},
    color: {type: "diverging", scheme: "RdBu", domain: [-12, 12], legend: false},
    marks
  });

  // ── D3 overlay for draggable point ──────────────────────────────────
  const {xScale, yScale} = plotScales(plot);
  const g = d3.select(plot).append("g");
  let cur = {u: pt1_1.u, v: pt1_1.v};

  const dot = g.append("circle").attr("r", 6)
    .attr("fill", "black").attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .style("cursor", "grab");
  const label = g.append("text")
    .attr("font-size", 12).attr("font-weight", 600).attr("text-anchor", "start");

  function update() {
    dot.attr("cx", xScale(cur.u)).attr("cy", yScale(cur.v));
    label.attr("x", xScale(cur.u) + 12).attr("y", yScale(cur.v) - 14)
      .text(`(${cur.u.toFixed(1)}, ${cur.v.toFixed(1)})  N = ${(cur.u*cur.u - c*cur.v*cur.v).toFixed(2)}`);
  }

  dot.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
      cur = {u: snap(xScale.invert(px), -D, D), v: snap(yScale.invert(py), -D, D)};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable pt1_1 = {...cur};
    })
  );

  update();
  return plot;
}
```

:::

## 2. Zero divisors appear immediately

The first test multiplication is the obvious one:

$$
(1+t)(1-t)=1-t^2=0.
$$

So two nonzero elements multiply to zero.

That already marks a decisive break from the complex numbers. In $\mathbb C$, nonzero elements never annihilate one another. Here they do, immediately. So whatever this algebra is, it is not a field. It is doing something more decomposed—something split.

And once the factors $1+t$ and $1-t$ appear, it is hard not to divide them by $2$ and see what happens. Define

$$
e_+=\frac{1+t}{2},
\qquad
e_- = \frac{1-t}{2}.
$$

Now compute:

$$
e_+^2=\frac{(1+t)^2}{4}=\frac{1+2t+t^2}{4}=\frac{2+2t}{4}=\frac{1+t}{2}=e_+,
$$

and similarly,

$$
e_-^2=e_-.
$$

Also,

$$
e_+e_- = \frac{(1+t)(1-t)}{4}=0.
$$

And finally,

$$
e_++e_-=1,
\qquad
e_+-e_-=t.
$$

This is the moment when the algebra suddenly becomes transparent. The new basis elements are

- idempotent: $e_\pm^2=e_\pm$,
- orthogonal under multiplication: $e_+e_-=0$,
- and they sum to the identity: $e_++e_-=1$.

I had started with a naive sign-flip experiment, and now a remarkably rigid structure had appeared almost uninvited.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: multiplication and the collapse to zero.** Drag the two factors $x$ (blue) and $y$ (red) on the split-complex plane. Their product $xy$ is shown in black. Try placing $x$ near the line $u = v$ and $y$ near $u = -v$. Watch the product shrink toward the origin — two perfectly nonzero elements annihilating each other. The norm bars below show how each element's norm $u^2 - v^2$ determines invertibility: when a norm crosses zero, you're on a zero-divisor line.

```{ojs}
mutable ptX = ({u: 1.8, v: 0.6})
mutable ptY = ({u: 0.5, v: -1.2})
```

```{ojs}
viz1_2 = {
  const D = 4;
  let curX = {u: ptX.u, v: ptX.v};
  let curY = {u: ptY.u, v: ptY.v};

  // ── Static background plot ──────────────────────────────────────────
  const plot = Plot.plot({
    width: 540, height: 540,
    grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, D], label: "u"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "v"},
    marks: [
      Plot.rect([{x1: -D, x2: 0, y1: 0, y2: D}],
        {x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y1: "y1", y2: "y2", fill: COL.hyper, opacity: 0.06}),
      Plot.rect([{x1: 0, x2: D, y1: -D, y2: 0}],
        {x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y1: "y1", y2: "y2", fill: COL.hyper, opacity: 0.06}),
      Plot.rect([{x1: 0, x2: D, y1: 0, y2: D}],
        {x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y1: "y1", y2: "y2", fill: COL.complex, opacity: 0.06}),
      Plot.rect([{x1: -D, x2: 0, y1: -D, y2: 0}],
        {x1: "x1", x2: "x2", y1: "y1", y2: "y2", fill: COL.complex, opacity: 0.06}),
      Plot.line([[-D, -D], [D, D]], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
      Plot.line([[-D, D], [D, -D]], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
      Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
      Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
    ]
  });

  const {xScale, yScale} = plotScales(plot);
  const svg = d3.select(plot);
  const ox = xScale(0), oy = yScale(0);

  // ── Arrowhead markers ───────────────────────────────────────────────
  const defs = svg.append("defs");
  const addMarker = (id, color) => {
    defs.append("marker").attr("id", id)
      .attr("viewBox", "0 0 10 10").attr("refX", 8).attr("refY", 5)
      .attr("markerWidth", 6).attr("markerHeight", 6)
      .attr("orient", "auto-start-reverse")
      .append("path").attr("d", "M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z").attr("fill", color);
  };
  addMarker("arr12-prod", "#111");
  addMarker("arr12-x", COL.complex);
  addMarker("arr12-y", COL.hyper);

  // ── Dynamic overlay ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const g = svg.append("g");

  const arrowProd = g.append("line")
    .attr("stroke", "#111").attr("stroke-width", 2)
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr12-prod)");
  const arrowX = g.append("line")
    .attr("stroke", COL.complex).attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "4")
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr12-x)");
  const arrowY = g.append("line")
    .attr("stroke", COL.hyper).attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .attr("stroke-dasharray", "4")
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr12-y)");

  const dotX = g.append("circle").attr("r", 7)
    .attr("fill", COL.complex).attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .style("cursor", "grab");
  const dotY = g.append("circle").attr("r", 7)
    .attr("fill", COL.hyper).attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .style("cursor", "grab");
  const dotP = g.append("circle").attr("r", 2)
    .attr("fill", "#111").attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5);

  const lblX = g.append("text")
    .attr("fill", COL.complex).attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 12);
  const lblY = g.append("text")
    .attr("fill", COL.hyper).attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 12);
  const lblP = g.append("text")
    .attr("fill", "#111").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 12);

  // ── Norm bars (persistent DOM for live updates) ─────────────────────
  const barW = 520, barH = 28, maxN = 12, mid = barW / 2;

  function makeBar(label, color) {
    const div = document.createElement("div");
    div.style.margin = "4px 0";
    div.innerHTML = `<div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:8px;">
      <span style="width:90px;font-size:0.85em;font-weight:600;color:${color}">${label}</span>
      <svg width="${barW}" height="${barH}">
        <rect x="0" y="0" width="${barW}" height="${barH}" fill="#f3f4f6" rx="4"/>
        <line x1="${mid}" y1="0" x2="${mid}" y2="${barH}" stroke="#9ca3af" stroke-dasharray="3"/>
        <rect class="fill" x="${mid}" y="3" width="0" height="${barH-6}" fill="${color}" opacity="0.6" rx="3"/>
        <text class="val" x="${mid}" y="${barH/2+4}" font-size="11" fill="#374151">0</text>
      </svg></div>`;
    return div;
  }

  function setBar(bar, val) {
    const frac = Math.max(-1, Math.min(1, val / maxN));
    const bx = frac >= 0 ? mid : mid + frac * mid;
    const bw = Math.abs(frac) * mid;
    const fill = bar.querySelector(".fill");
    const txt  = bar.querySelector(".val");
    fill.setAttribute("x", bx);
    fill.setAttribute("width", bw);
    txt.setAttribute("x", mid + frac * mid + (frac >= 0 ? 4 : -4));
    txt.setAttribute("text-anchor", frac >= 0 ? "start" : "end");
    txt.textContent = val.toFixed(2);
  }

  const barX = makeBar("N(x) =", COL.complex);
  const barY = makeBar("N(y) =", COL.hyper);
  const barP = makeBar("N(xy) =", "#111");

  // ── Label offset: perpendicular to the vector from origin ───────────
  function labelPos(sx, sy) {
    const dx = sx - ox, dy = sy - oy;
    const len = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);
    if (len < 1) return {x: sx + 12, y: sy - 12, anchor: "start"};
    // Two perpendicular candidates (CCW and CW rotations)
    const p1x = -dy / len, p1y = dx / len;
    const p2x = dy / len, p2y = -dx / len;
    // Prefer the perpendicular pointing more rightward; tiebreak: more upward
    const pick1 = p1x > p2x || (p1x === p2x && p1y < p2y);
    const nx = pick1 ? p1x : p2x, ny = pick1 ? p1y : p2y;
    const off = 18;
    return {
      x: sx + nx * off,
      y: sy + ny * off + 4,
      anchor: nx > 0.1 ? "start" : nx < -0.1 ? "end" : "middle"
    };
  }

  // ── Live update ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  function update() {
    const prod = scMul(curX, curY);

    arrowProd.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(prod.u)).attr("y2", yScale(prod.v));
    arrowX.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(curX.u)).attr("y2", yScale(curX.v));
    arrowY.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(curY.u)).attr("y2", yScale(curY.v));

    dotX.attr("cx", xScale(curX.u)).attr("cy", yScale(curX.v));
    dotY.attr("cx", xScale(curY.u)).attr("cy", yScale(curY.v));
    dotP.attr("cx", xScale(prod.u)).attr("cy", yScale(prod.v));

    const posX = labelPos(xScale(curX.u), yScale(curX.v));
    lblX.attr("x", posX.x).attr("y", posX.y).attr("text-anchor", posX.anchor)
      .text(`x = ${curX.u.toFixed(1)} + ${curX.v.toFixed(1)}t`);
    const posY = labelPos(xScale(curY.u), yScale(curY.v));
    lblY.attr("x", posY.x).attr("y", posY.y).attr("text-anchor", posY.anchor)
      .text(`y = ${curY.u.toFixed(1)} + ${curY.v.toFixed(1)}t`);
    const posP = labelPos(xScale(prod.u), yScale(prod.v));
    lblP.attr("x", posP.x).attr("y", posP.y).attr("text-anchor", posP.anchor)
      .text(`xy = ${prod.u.toFixed(2)} + ${prod.v.toFixed(2)}t`);

    setBar(barX, normSC(curX.u, curX.v));
    setBar(barY, normSC(curY.u, curY.v));
    setBar(barP, normSC(prod.u, prod.v));
  }

  // ── Drag handlers ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  dotX.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
      curX = {u: snap(xScale.invert(px), -D, D), v: snap(yScale.invert(py), -D, D)};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable ptX = {...curX};
    })
  );

  dotY.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
      curY = {u: snap(xScale.invert(px), -D, D), v: snap(yScale.invert(py), -D, D)};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable ptY = {...curY};
    })
  );

  update();

  // ── Assemble ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const barsDiv = document.createElement("div");
  barsDiv.style.cssText = "max-width:620px;margin-top:8px;";
  barsDiv.append(barX, barY, barP);
  const out = document.createElement("div");
  out.append(plot, barsDiv);
  return out;
}
```

:::

## 3. Every element rewrites naturally in the idempotent basis

Since

$$
1=e_+ + e_-,
\qquad
t=e_+ - e_-,
$$

any element

$$
x=u+vt
$$

can be rewritten as

$$
x=u(e_++e_-)+v(e_+-e_-)
=(u+v)e_+ + (u-v)e_-.
$$

So if I define

$$
a=u+v,
\qquad
b=u-v,
$$

then every element takes the form

$$
x=ae_+ + be_-.
$$

This already suggests that the algebra is decomposing into two independent channels.

And now multiplication diagonalizes completely. If

$$
x=ae_+ + be_-,
\qquad
y=ce_+ + de_-,
$$

then, using only

$$
e_+^2=e_+,
\qquad
e_-^2=e_-,
\qquad
e_+e_-=0,
$$

we get

$$
xy=(ae_+ + be_-)(ce_+ + de_-)=ace_+ + bde_-.
$$

So in these coordinates, addition and multiplication become

$$
(a,b)+(c,d)=(a+c,b+d)
$$

and

$$
(a,b)(c,d)=(ac,bd).
$$

The algebra has diagonalized itself. That was the first moment it stopped feeling like a slightly odd cousin of $\mathbb C$ and started feeling like something built to split cleanly into independent parts.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: the multiplication machine.** Adjust $x$ and $y$ to see how multiplication works in both bases. On the left, the standard-basis multiplication has **four** terms — the cross terms entangle the two components. On the right, the idempotent-basis multiplication has only **two** independent channel multiplications; the cross terms are exactly zero. Both sides compute the same result.

```{ojs}
viewof mulInputs = Inputs.form({
  a1: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 2, step: 0.1, label: "a₁  (e₊ coeff of x)"}),
  b1: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "b₁  (e₋ coeff of x)"}),
  a2: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1.5, step: 0.1, label: "a₂  (e₊ coeff of y)"}),
  b2: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: -0.5, step: 0.1, label: "b₂  (e₋ coeff of y)"}),
})
```

```{ojs}
viz2_2 = {
  const {a1, b1, a2, b2} = mulInputs;
  // Convert to (u,v) coords
  const {u: u1, v: v1} = toUV(a1, b1);
  const {u: u2, v: v2} = toUV(a2, b2);

  // Standard basis: (u1+v1t)(u2+v2t) = (u1u2+v1v2) + (u1v2+v1u2)t
  const s_uu = u1 * u2, s_vv = v1 * v2, s_uv = u1 * v2, s_vu = v1 * u2;
  const res_u = s_uu + s_vv, res_v = s_uv + s_vu;

  // Idempotent basis: (a1 e+)(a2 e+) = a1a2 e+,  (b1 e-)(b2 e-) = b1b2 e-
  const res_a = a1 * a2, res_b = b1 * b2;

  const cell = (val, bg, label, gray) => html`<div class="foil-cell" style="background:${gray ? '#f3f4f6' : bg};opacity:${gray ? 0.4 : 1}">
    <div style="font-size:0.75em;color:#6b7280">${label}</div>
    <div style="font-size:1.1em;font-weight:600">${gray ? '0' : val.toFixed(2)}</div>
  </div>`;

  const panel = (title, color, grid, result) => html`<div style="flex:1;min-width:280px">
    <div style="text-align:center;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:6px;color:${color}">${title}</div>
    <div class="foil-grid">${grid}</div>
    <div style="text-align:center;margin-top:10px;padding:8px;background:#f9fafb;border-radius:6px;font-weight:600">
      ${result}
    </div>
  </div>`;

  const stdGrid = html`
    ${cell(s_uu, '#dbeafe', 'u₁u₂', false)}
    ${cell(s_uv, '#fef3c7', 'u₁v₂ · t', false)}
    ${cell(s_vu, '#fef3c7', 'v₁u₂ · t', false)}
    ${cell(s_vv, '#dbeafe', 'v₁v₂ · t² = v₁v₂', false)}
  `;

  const idGrid = html`
    ${cell(res_a, '#d1fae5', 'a₁a₂ · e₊', false)}
    ${cell(0, '', 'a₁b₂ · e₊e₋', true)}
    ${cell(0, '', 'b₁a₂ · e₋e₊', true)}
    ${cell(res_b, '#ede9fe', 'b₁b₂ · e₋', false)}
  `;

  const fmtSgn = (v) => v >= 0 ? `+ ${Math.abs(v).toFixed(2)}` : `− ${Math.abs(v).toFixed(2)}`;

  return html`<div style="display:flex;gap:24px;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:center;">
    ${panel(
      "Standard basis",
      "#374151",
      stdGrid,
      html`= ${res_u.toFixed(2)} ${fmtSgn(res_v)}<i>t</i>`
    )}
    ${panel(
      "Idempotent basis",
      "#374151",
      idGrid,
      html`= <span style="color:${COL.ePlus}">${res_a.toFixed(2)} e₊</span> ${res_b >= 0 ? '+' : '−'} <span style="color:${COL.eMinus}">${Math.abs(res_b).toFixed(2)} e₋</span>`
    )}
  </div>
  <div style="text-align:center;margin-top:12px;font-size:0.9em;color:#6b7280">
    Cross-talk in standard basis: <strong>${Math.abs(s_uv + s_vu).toFixed(2)}</strong> &nbsp;|&nbsp;
    Cross-talk in idempotent basis: <strong style="color:${COL.ePlus}">0.00</strong> (always)
  </div>`;
}
```

:::

## 4. What the algebra actually is

At this point the abstract description becomes recognizable. The algebra I built is

$$
\mathbb R[t]/(t^2-1).
$$

Since

$$
t^2-1=(t-1)(t+1),
$$

and the two factors are coprime, the Chinese remainder theorem gives

$$
\mathbb R[t]/(t^2-1)
\cong
\mathbb R[t]/(t-1)\times \mathbb R[t]/(t+1)
\cong
\mathbb R\times\mathbb R.
$$

Concretely, the isomorphism is

$$
u+vt \longmapsto (u+v,u-v).
$$

So this strange-looking algebra is not strange at all once the right coordinates are chosen. It is simply $\mathbb R^2$ with coordinatewise multiplication.

As I learned after doing the whole exploration, this is the classical algebra usually called the **[split-complex numbers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Split-complex_number?wprov=sfti1#)**, though it also goes by names such as the **hyperbolic numbers**, **double numbers**, or **perplex numbers**.

So the experiment had not produced an exotic new object at all. It had backed, almost accidentally, into a classical one. That only made me want to keep going.

### Inverses and zero divisors

In the $(a,b)$ coordinates, inversion is immediate. The element $(a,b)$ is invertible exactly when both coordinates are nonzero, and in that case

$$
(a,b)^{-1}=\left(\frac1a,\frac1b\right).
$$

Translating this back into the $u+vt$ form gives

$$
(u+vt)^{-1}=\frac{u-vt}{u^2-v^2},
$$

provided that

$$
u^2-v^2\neq 0.
$$

So the zero divisors are precisely the elements satisfying

$$
u^2-v^2=0,
$$

that is,

$$
u=v
\qquad \text{or} \qquad
u=-v.
$$

Geometrically, in the $(u,v)$-plane, the singular set is the union of the two lines

$$
u=v
\qquad \text{and} \qquad
u=-v.
$$

This is one of the places where the familiar hyperbolic geometry of the split-complex numbers begins to peek through.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: the isomorphism in action.** These two planes are linked by the map $u + vt \mapsto (a,b) = (u+v, \, u-v)$. Drag the point in **either** plane and watch the other update. In the left plane, the zero-divisor lines $u = \pm v$ look like mysterious diagonals. In the right plane, they are simply **the coordinate axes** $a = 0$ and $b = 0$. The norm $N = u^2 - v^2$ in the left plane equals $ab$ in the right. What looks singular on the left is entirely ordinary on the right.

```{ojs}
mutable splitPt = ({u: 1.8, v: 0.6})
```

```{ojs}
viz2_1 = {
  const D = 4;
  let cur = {u: splitPt.u, v: splitPt.v};

  // ── Generate level curves (static background) ────────────────────────
  const sRange = Array.from({length: 301}, (_, i) => -3 + i * 0.02);
  const levels = [0.5, 1, 2, 4];

  const uvCurves = [];
  for (const k of levels) {
    const sk = Math.sqrt(k);
    uvCurves.push({pts: sRange.map(s => ({x: sk*Math.cosh(s), y: sk*Math.sinh(s)})), stroke: COL.complex, k});
    uvCurves.push({pts: sRange.map(s => ({x:-sk*Math.cosh(s), y: sk*Math.sinh(s)})), stroke: COL.complex, k});
    uvCurves.push({pts: sRange.map(s => ({x: sk*Math.sinh(s), y: sk*Math.cosh(s)})), stroke: COL.hyper, k});
    uvCurves.push({pts: sRange.map(s => ({x: sk*Math.sinh(s), y:-sk*Math.cosh(s)})), stroke: COL.hyper, k});
  }

  const abCurves = [];
  const aRange = Array.from({length: 601}, (_, i) => -D + i * (2*D)/600).filter(x => Math.abs(x) > 0.05);
  for (const k of levels) {
    abCurves.push({pts: aRange.filter(aa => Math.abs(k/aa) < D).map(aa => ({x: aa, y: k/aa})), k, sign: "+"});
    abCurves.push({pts: aRange.filter(aa => Math.abs(k/aa) < D).map(aa => ({x: aa, y: -k/aa})), k, sign: "-"});
  }

  // ── Left plot (static marks only) ─────────────────────────────────────
  const leftMarks = [
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
    Plot.line([[-D,-D],[D,D]], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.line([[-D,D],[D,-D]], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeDasharray: "6,3", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
  ];
  for (const c of uvCurves) {
    const visible = c.pts.filter(d => Math.abs(d.x) <= D && Math.abs(d.y) <= D);
    if (visible.length > 2)
      leftMarks.push(Plot.line(visible, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: c.stroke, strokeWidth: 1, opacity: 0.25}));
  }
  leftMarks.push(
    Plot.arrow([{x1:0,y1:0,x2:0.5,y2:0.5}], {x1:"x1",y1:"y1",x2:"x2",y2:"y2", stroke: COL.ePlus, strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.arrow([{x1:0,y1:0,x2:0.5,y2:-0.5}], {x1:"x1",y1:"y1",x2:"x2",y2:"y2", stroke: COL.eMinus, strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.text([{x:0.65,y:0.65}], {x:"x",y:"y",text:["e₊"],fill:COL.ePlus,fontWeight:"bold",fontSize:13}),
    Plot.text([{x:0.65,y:-0.65}], {x:"x",y:"y",text:["e₋"],fill:COL.eMinus,fontWeight:"bold",fontSize:13}),
  );
  const leftPlot = Plot.plot({
    width: 380, height: 380, grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, D], label: "u"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "v"},
    marks: leftMarks
  });

  // ── Right plot (static marks only) ────────────────────────────────────
  const rightMarks = [
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: COL.zd, strokeWidth: 2}),
  ];
  for (const c of abCurves) {
    const pos = c.pts.filter(d => d.x > 0);
    const neg = c.pts.filter(d => d.x < 0);
    const color = c.sign === "+" ? COL.complex : COL.hyper;
    if (pos.length > 2)
      rightMarks.push(Plot.line(pos, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: color, strokeWidth: 1, opacity: 0.25}));
    if (neg.length > 2)
      rightMarks.push(Plot.line(neg, {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: color, strokeWidth: 1, opacity: 0.25}));
  }
  rightMarks.push(
    Plot.line([[-D,-D],[D,D]], {stroke: "#bbb", strokeDasharray: "2", strokeWidth: 0.8}),
    Plot.text([{x:D-0.5,y:D-0.3}], {x:"x",y:"y",text:["a=b (real axis)"],fill:"#999",fontSize:10}),
    Plot.arrow([{x1:0,y1:0,x2:1,y2:1}], {x1:"x1",y1:"y1",x2:"x2",y2:"y2", stroke: "#555", strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.arrow([{x1:0,y1:0,x2:1,y2:-1}], {x1:"x1",y1:"y1",x2:"x2",y2:"y2", stroke: "#555", strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.text([{x:1.25,y:1.25}], {x:"x",y:"y",text:["1"],fill:"#555",fontWeight:"bold",fontSize:13}),
    Plot.text([{x:1.25,y:-1.25}], {x:"x",y:"y",text:["t"],fill:"#555",fontWeight:"bold",fontSize:13}),
  );
  const rightPlot = Plot.plot({
    width: 380, height: 380, grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, D], label: "a  (= u + v)"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "b  (= u − v)"},
    marks: rightMarks
  });

  // ── D3 overlays for both panels ───────────────────────────────────────
  const lS = plotScales(leftPlot), rS = plotScales(rightPlot);
  const lG = d3.select(leftPlot).append("g");
  const rG = d3.select(rightPlot).append("g");

  // Left overlay: projection lines, axis projections, dot, label
  const lProjPlusLine = lG.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.ePlus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const lProjMinusLine = lG.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.eMinus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const lProjULine = lG.append("line").attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const lProjVLine = lG.append("line").attr("stroke", "#888").attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const lDotU = lG.append("circle").attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#555");
  const lDotV = lG.append("circle").attr("r", 4).attr("fill", "#555");
  const lDot = lG.append("circle").attr("r", 6).attr("fill", "#111").attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5).style("cursor", "grab");
  const lLabel = lG.append("text").attr("font-size", 11).attr("font-weight", 600);
  const lLabelU = lG.append("text").attr("fill", "#555").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 11);
  const lLabelV = lG.append("text").attr("fill", "#555").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 11).attr("text-anchor", "end");

  // Right overlay: projection lines, axis dots, dot, labels
  const rProjALine = rG.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.ePlus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.6);
  const rProjBLine = rG.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.eMinus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "3").attr("opacity", 0.6);
  const rDotA = rG.append("circle").attr("r", 4).attr("fill", COL.ePlus);
  const rDotB = rG.append("circle").attr("r", 4).attr("fill", COL.eMinus);
  const rDot = rG.append("circle").attr("r", 6).attr("fill", "#111").attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5).style("cursor", "grab");
  const rLabel = rG.append("text").attr("font-size", 11).attr("font-weight", 600);
  const rLabelA = rG.append("text").attr("fill", COL.ePlus).attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 11);
  const rLabelB = rG.append("text").attr("fill", COL.eMinus).attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 11).attr("text-anchor", "end");

  // Bottom annotation
  const annoDiv = document.createElement("div");
  annoDiv.style.cssText = "text-align:center;margin-top:6px;font-size:0.9em";

  // ── Shared update function ────────────────────────────────────────────
  function update() {
    const {a, b} = toAB(cur.u, cur.v);
    const N = normSC(cur.u, cur.v);
    const projPlus  = {x: (cur.u+cur.v)/2, y: (cur.u+cur.v)/2};
    const projMinus = {x: (cur.u-cur.v)/2, y: -(cur.u-cur.v)/2};

    // Left panel
    const lx = lS.xScale, ly = lS.yScale;
    lProjPlusLine.attr("x1", lx(projPlus.x)).attr("y1", ly(projPlus.y))
      .attr("x2", lx(cur.u)).attr("y2", ly(cur.v));
    lProjMinusLine.attr("x1", lx(projMinus.x)).attr("y1", ly(projMinus.y))
      .attr("x2", lx(cur.u)).attr("y2", ly(cur.v));
    lProjULine.attr("x1", lx(cur.u)).attr("y1", ly(cur.v)).attr("x2", lx(cur.u)).attr("y2", ly(0));
    lProjVLine.attr("x1", lx(cur.u)).attr("y1", ly(cur.v)).attr("x2", lx(0)).attr("y2", ly(cur.v));
    lDotU.attr("cx", lx(cur.u)).attr("cy", ly(0));
    lDotV.attr("cx", lx(0)).attr("cy", ly(cur.v));
    lDot.attr("cx", lx(cur.u)).attr("cy", ly(cur.v));
    lLabel.attr("x", lx(cur.u) + 10).attr("y", ly(cur.v) - 12)
      .text(`N=${N.toFixed(2)}`);
    lLabelU.attr("x", lx(cur.u)).attr("y", ly(0) + 14).text(`u=${cur.u.toFixed(1)}`);
    lLabelV.attr("x", lx(0) - 14).attr("y", ly(cur.v)).text(`v=${cur.v.toFixed(1)}`);

    // Right panel
    const rx = rS.xScale, ry = rS.yScale;
    rProjALine.attr("x1", rx(a)).attr("y1", ry(b)).attr("x2", rx(a)).attr("y2", ry(0));
    rProjBLine.attr("x1", rx(a)).attr("y1", ry(b)).attr("x2", rx(0)).attr("y2", ry(b));
    rDotA.attr("cx", rx(a)).attr("cy", ry(0));
    rDotB.attr("cx", rx(0)).attr("cy", ry(b));
    rDot.attr("cx", rx(a)).attr("cy", ry(b));
    rLabel.attr("x", rx(a) + 10).attr("y", ry(b) - 12)
      .text(`ab=${(a*b).toFixed(2)}`);
    rLabelA.attr("x", rx(a)).attr("y", ry(0) + 14).text(`a=${a.toFixed(1)}`);
    rLabelB.attr("x", rx(0) - 14).attr("y", ry(b)).text(`b=${b.toFixed(1)}`);

    // Annotation
    annoDiv.innerHTML = `<span style="color:${COL.ePlus};font-weight:bold">a = ${a.toFixed(2)}</span> &nbsp;&nbsp; <span style="color:${COL.eMinus};font-weight:bold">b = ${b.toFixed(2)}</span> &nbsp;&nbsp; N = u² − v² = ab = <b>${N.toFixed(2)}</b>`;
  }

  // ── Drag handlers for both panels ─────────────────────────────────────
  lDot.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, leftPlot);
      cur = {u: snap(lS.xScale.invert(px), -D, D), v: snap(lS.yScale.invert(py), -D, D)};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable splitPt = {...cur};
    })
  );

  rDot.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, rightPlot);
      const newA = snap(rS.xScale.invert(px), -D, D);
      const newB = snap(rS.yScale.invert(py), -D, D);
      const uv = toUV(newA, newB);
      cur = {u: uv.u, v: uv.v};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable splitPt = {...cur};
    })
  );

  update();

  // ── Assemble layout ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const leftDiv = document.createElement("div");
  const leftTitle = document.createElement("div");
  leftTitle.className = "viz-label";
  leftTitle.textContent = "Standard coordinates  (u, v)";
  leftDiv.append(leftTitle, leftPlot);

  const rightDiv = document.createElement("div");
  const rightTitle = document.createElement("div");
  rightTitle.className = "viz-label";
  rightTitle.textContent = "Idempotent coordinates  (a, b) = (u+v, u−v)";
  rightDiv.append(rightTitle, rightPlot);

  const panel = document.createElement("div");
  panel.className = "dual-panel";
  panel.append(leftDiv, rightDiv);

  const out = document.createElement("div");
  out.append(panel, annoDiv);
  return out;
}
```

:::

### Functions act coordinatewise

Once the idempotent basis is in place, another pleasant fact becomes almost automatic. For any polynomial $p$, one has

$$
p(a\operatorname{e}_+ + b \operatorname{e}_-)=p(a)\operatorname{e}_+ + p(b)\operatorname{e}_-.
$$

The reason is simple: powers never mix,

$$
(a \operatorname e_+ + b \operatorname e_-)^n = a^n \operatorname e_+ + b^n \operatorname e_-,
$$

because all cross-terms die. And whenever a function defined by a power-series converges, the same pointwise rule continues to hold.

So, for example,

$$
\exp{(a \operatorname e_+ +\, b \operatorname e_-)}=\exp(a) \operatorname e_+ +\, \exp(b) \operatorname e_-.
$$

Returning to the $u+vt$ coordinates, where $a=u+v$ and $b=u-v$, this becomes

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{u+vt}
&=e^{u+v} \operatorname e_+ + e^{u-v} \operatorname e_- \\
&=e^u\bigl(e^v \operatorname e_+ + e^{-v} \operatorname e_-\bigr) \\
&=e^u\left(\cosh v + t\sinh v\right).
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the hyperbolic analogue of the familiar complex formula

$$
e^{x+yi}=e^x(\cos y + i\sin y).
$$

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: circle vs. hyperbola.** These two panels are controlled by the same parameter. On the left, the complex exponential $e^{i\theta}$ traces a **circle** — rotation. On the right, the split-complex exponential $e^{vt}$ traces a **hyperbola** — hyperbolic rotation. Toggle "Show trails" to see the full trajectory at once, abstracting over the parameter. Below each panel, the idempotent decomposition shows what's happening to each channel: in the split-complex case, the channels are simply $e^v$ and $e^{-v}$ — one grows, the other shrinks.

```{ojs}
viewof eulerParam = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.02, label: "Parameter  θ / v", width: 480})
viewof showTrails = Inputs.toggle({label: "Show trails", value: false})
```

```{ojs}
viz4_2 = {
  const th = eulerParam;

  // Complex: e^{iθ} = cos θ + i sin θ
  const cx = Math.cos(th), cy = Math.sin(th);

  // Split-complex: e^{vt} = cosh v + t sinh v
  const su = Math.cosh(th), sv = Math.sinh(th);
  // In idempotent coords: a = e^v, b = e^{-v}
  const sa = Math.exp(th), sb = Math.exp(-th);

  // Trail data
  const trail = Array.from({length: 301}, (_, i) => {
    const t = -3 + i * 0.02;
    return {
      cx: Math.cos(t), cy: Math.sin(t),
      su: Math.cosh(t), sv: Math.sinh(t),
      t
    };
  });

  const D_c = 1.6, D_s = 12;

  const leftMarks = [
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}), Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
  ];
  if (showTrails)
    leftMarks.push(Plot.line(trail, {x: "cx", y: "cy", stroke: "#ddd", strokeWidth: 2}));
  leftMarks.push(
    // Unit circle
    Plot.line(trail, {x: "cx", y: "cy", stroke: COL.complex, strokeWidth: 1.5, opacity: 0.4}),
    // Current point
    Plot.dot([{x: cx, y: cy}], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: COL.complex, r: 7, stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    // Projections
    Plot.line([[cx, cy], [cx, 0]], {stroke: COL.complex, strokeDasharray: "3", opacity: 0.5}),
    Plot.line([[cx, cy], [0, cy]], {stroke: COL.complex, strokeDasharray: "3", opacity: 0.5}),
    Plot.text([{x: cx, y: cy}], {x: "x", y: "y",
      text: [`(${cx.toFixed(2)}, ${cy.toFixed(2)})`], dx: 10, dy: -10, fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 600}),
  );

  const leftPlot = Plot.plot({
    width: 320, height: 320, grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D_c, D_c], label: "cos θ"},
    y: {domain: [-D_c, D_c], label: "sin θ"},
    marks: leftMarks
  });

  const rightMarks = [
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}), Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
  ];
  if (showTrails) {
    const trailPos = trail.filter(d => d.su <= D_s && Math.abs(d.sv) <= D_s);
    rightMarks.push(Plot.line(trailPos, {x: "su", y: "sv", stroke: "#ddd", strokeWidth: 2}));
  }
  rightMarks.push(
    // Right branch of hyperbola
    Plot.line(trail.filter(d => d.su <= D_s), {x: "su", y: "sv", stroke: COL.hyper, strokeWidth: 1.5, opacity: 0.4}),
    Plot.dot([{x: su, y: sv}], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: COL.hyper, r: 7, stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.line([[su, sv], [su, 0]], {stroke: COL.hyper, strokeDasharray: "3", opacity: 0.5}),
    Plot.line([[su, sv], [0, sv]], {stroke: COL.hyper, strokeDasharray: "3", opacity: 0.5}),
    Plot.text([{x: su, y: sv}], {x: "x", y: "y",
      text: [`(${su.toFixed(2)}, ${sv.toFixed(2)})`], dx: 10, dy: -10, fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 600}),
  );

  const rightPlot = Plot.plot({
    width: 320, height: 320, grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D_s, D_s], label: "cosh v  (= 1 part)"},
    y: {domain: [-D_s, D_s], label: "sinh v  (= t part)"},
    marks: rightMarks
  });

  // Channel decomposition bars
  const channelBar = (label, val, color, max) => {
    const W = 180, H = 18;
    const frac = Math.min(Math.abs(val) / max, 1);
    const x = val >= 0 ? W/2 : W/2 - frac * W/2;
    const w = frac * W/2;
    return html`<div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:6px;margin:2px 0">
      <span style="width:40px;font-size:0.78em;color:${color};font-weight:600">${label}</span>
      <svg width="${W}" height="${H}">
        <rect width="${W}" height="${H}" fill="#f5f5f5" rx="3"/>
        <line x1="${W/2}" y1="0" x2="${W/2}" y2="${H}" stroke="#ccc"/>
        <rect x="${x}" y="2" width="${w}" height="${H-4}" fill="${color}" opacity="0.6" rx="2"/>
      </svg>
      <span style="font-size:0.78em;width:50px">${val.toFixed(2)}</span>
    </div>`;
  };

  return html`<div class="dual-panel">
    <div>
      <div class="viz-label">Complex: ${tex`e^{i\theta} = \cos\theta + i\sin\theta`}</div>
      ${leftPlot}
    </div>
    <div>
      <div class="viz-label">Split-complex: ${tex`e^{vt} = \cosh v + t\sinh v`}</div>
      ${rightPlot}
      <div style="margin-top:8px">
        ${channelBar("e₊", sa, COL.ePlus, 20)}
        ${channelBar("e₋", sb, COL.eMinus, 20)}
        <div style="font-size:0.75em;color:#999;margin-top:2px">
          Channels: (e<sup>v</sup>, e<sup>−v</sup>) = (${sa.toFixed(2)}, ${sb.toFixed(2)})
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

:::

And the idempotents do something else neat as well: they isolate the two coordinates. If

$$
x=a \operatorname e_+ + b \operatorname e_-,
$$

then

$$
\operatorname e_+x=a \operatorname e_+,
\qquad
\operatorname e_-x= b \operatorname e_-.
$$

Likewise, for any coordinatewise-defined function $f$,

$$
\operatorname e_+f(a,b)=f(a) \operatorname e_+,
\qquad
\operatorname e_-f(a,b)=f(b) \operatorname e_-.
$$

So the idempotents are not just basis vectors. They are also projection operators onto the two independent channels.

## 5. The right conceptual name: complete orthogonal idempotents

The pair $e_+,e_-$ is not just a lucky trick. It belongs to a general pattern.

A family $e_1,\dots,e_n$ in an algebra is called a **complete set of pairwise orthogonal idempotents** if

$$
e_i^2=e_i,
\qquad
e_ie_j=0 \text{ for } i\neq j,
\qquad
e_1+\cdots+e_n=1.
$$

That is exactly what happened here.

This is not the same thing as an orthonormal basis in an inner-product space, because the orthogonality here is multiplicative rather than metric. But the analogy is strong. An orthonormal basis diagonalizes an inner product; a complete set of orthogonal idempotents diagonalizes multiplication.

The formulas even rhyme:

$$
\langle v_i,v_j\rangle = \delta_{ij}
$$

versus

$$
e_ie_j=\delta_{ij}e_i.
$$

That was one of the most satisfying moments in the exploration: this basis felt like an orthogonal basis, but for algebra itself.

## 6. The surprise hiding underneath: this is really a function algebra

The coordinatewise multiplication law suggested a different interpretation. Consider the two-point set

$$
S=\{+,-\}.
$$

The algebra of real-valued functions on $S$ is just

$$
\mathbb R^S \cong \mathbb R\times\mathbb R,
$$

with pointwise addition and multiplication.

Now take the two singleton indicators

$$
\delta_+ := \mathbf 1_{\{+\}},
\qquad
\delta_- := \mathbf 1_{\{-\}}.
$$

They satisfy

$$
\delta_+^2=\delta_+,
\qquad
\delta_-^2=\delta_-,
$$

$$
\delta_+\delta_-=0,
\qquad
\delta_+ + \delta_- = 1.
$$

So the basis $e_+,e_-$ is doing exactly what the indicator basis does in a two-point function algebra.

This was the point where the experiment took a turn I had not anticipated. It was the first real “oh!” moment for me. I had not merely found a convenient basis for a funny number system. I had stumbled into the algebra of functions on a finite set.

And once that interpretation appears, the general pattern is hard to miss. For any finite set $S$ and any field $F$, the algebra

$$
F^S
$$

of all $F$-valued functions on $S$ has a canonical basis given by the singleton indicators

$$
\delta_s := \mathbf 1_{\{s\}}, \qquad s\in S.
$$

These satisfy

$$
\delta_s^2=\delta_s,
\qquad
\delta_s\delta_t=0 \text{ for } s\neq t,
\qquad
\sum_{s\in S}\delta_s=1.
$$

Every function expands as

$$
f=\sum_{s\in S} f(s)\,\delta_s,
$$

and multiplication is pointwise:

$$
(fg)(s)=f(s)g(s).
$$

And so is addition:

$$
(f+g)(s)=f(s)+g(s).
$$

So the phenomenon I had rediscovered from the single relation $t^2=1$ was not a special trick at all. It was the smallest nontrivial example of a general principle:

> Function algebras on finite sets come with a canonical multiplicatively orthogonal basis of idempotents.

Even better, the coefficients in that basis are not mysterious. They are just the function values. Multiplying by one of the basis idempotents isolates a single coordinate:

$$
\delta_s f = f(s)\,\delta_s.
$$

So the same objects play two roles at once: they are the atomic basis vectors, and they are the algebraic devices that pick out individual coordinates.

In retrospect that makes the original split-complex discovery feel even cleaner. The elements $e_+$ and $e_-$ were not merely a convenient change of basis. They were the two point-masses on a two-point space, and multiplication had become pointwise multiplication because the algebra had quietly turned into the algebra of functions on that space.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: the function algebra in action.** A split-complex number is secretly a function on the two-point set $\{+, -\}$. Adjust the sliders to set $f(+)$ and $f(-)$ for two functions, or equivalently their $e_+$ and $e_-$ coordinates. Multiplication is **pointwise**: each bar is multiplied independently, with zero cross-talk. The indicator functions $\delta_+$ and $\delta_-$ are exactly the idempotents $e_+$ and $e_-$ — each "lights up" exactly one point.

```{ojs}
viewof fnInputs = Inputs.form({
  fp: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 2.0, step: 0.1, label: "f(+)  =  a"}),
  fm: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: -1.0, step: 0.1, label: "f(−)  =  b"}),
  gp: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1.5, step: 0.1, label: "g(+)  =  c"}),
  gm: Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 0.8, step: 0.1, label: "g(−)  =  d"}),
})
```

```{ojs}
viz3_1 = {
  const {fp, fm, gp, gm} = fnInputs;
  const hp = fp * gp, hm = fm * gm;   // pointwise product
  const maxH = 3.5;

  const barChart = (vals, labels, colors, title) => {
    const W = 160, H = 180, pad = 30, barW = 40, mid = H / 2 + 10;
    const scale = (H - 2 * pad) / (2 * maxH);
    return html`<div style="text-align:center">
      <div style="font-weight:600;font-size:0.85em;margin-bottom:4px">${title}</div>
      <svg width="${W}" height="${H}">
        <line x1="0" y1="${mid}" x2="${W}" y2="${mid}" stroke="#ccc"/>
        ${vals.map((v, i) => {
          const x = 20 + i * (barW + 30);
          const h = Math.abs(v) * scale;
          const y = v >= 0 ? mid - h : mid;
          return html`<g>
            <rect x="${x}" y="${y}" width="${barW}" height="${Math.max(h, 1)}"
                  fill="${colors[i]}" opacity="0.7" rx="3"/>
            <text x="${x + barW/2}" y="${v >= 0 ? y - 4 : y + h + 13}"
                  text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="600"
                  fill="${colors[i]}">${v.toFixed(1)}</text>
            <text x="${x + barW/2}" y="${H - 2}"
                  text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="#6b7280">${labels[i]}</text>
          </g>`;
        })}
      </svg>
    </div>`;
  };

  const colP = COL.ePlus, colM = COL.eMinus;

  const fChart = barChart([fp, fm], ["+", "−"], [colP, colM], "f");
  const gChart = barChart([gp, gm], ["+", "−"], [colP, colM], "g");
  const hChart = barChart([hp, hm], ["+", "−"], [colP, colM], "f · g");

  // Indicator functions
  const d1 = barChart([1, 0], ["+", "−"], [colP, "#ddd"], "δ₊ = e₊");
  const d2 = barChart([0, 1], ["+", "−"], ["#ddd", colM], "δ₋ = e₋");

  return html`<div>
    <div style="display:flex;gap:10px;align-items:end;justify-content:center;flex-wrap:wrap">
      ${fChart}
      <div style="font-size:1.5em;padding-bottom:60px">×</div>
      ${gChart}
      <div style="font-size:1.5em;padding-bottom:60px">=</div>
      ${hChart}
    </div>
    <div style="text-align:center;margin:12px 0 4px;font-size:0.9em;color:#6b7280">
      Each bar is multiplied <strong>independently</strong>.
      <span style="color:${colP}">f(+)·g(+) = ${fp.toFixed(1)}·${gp.toFixed(1)} = ${hp.toFixed(2)}</span> &nbsp;|&nbsp;
      <span style="color:${colM}">f(−)·g(−) = ${fm.toFixed(1)}·${gm.toFixed(1)} = ${hm.toFixed(2)}</span>
    </div>
    <details style="margin-top:16px">
      <summary style="cursor:pointer;font-size:0.9em;color:#6b7280">Show indicator functions (the atomic basis)</summary>
      <div style="display:flex;gap:24px;justify-content:center;margin-top:8px">
        ${d1} ${d2}
      </div>
      <div style="text-align:center;margin-top:8px;font-size:0.9em">
        ${tex`f = \color{${colP}}{${fp.toFixed(1)}} \cdot \delta_+ \;+\; \color{${colM}}{${fm.toFixed(1)}} \cdot \delta_- \;=\; \color{${colP}}{${fp.toFixed(1)}} \cdot e_+ \;+\; \color{${colM}}{${fm.toFixed(1)}} \cdot e_-`}
      </div>
    </details>
  </div>`;
}
```

:::

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: projection as filtering.** Multiplying by $e_+$ kills the $e_-$ channel and vice versa — exactly like an indicator function picking out a single evaluation point. Drag the point and watch the projections update. The green arrow is $e_+ \cdot x$ (only the $a$-component survives), and the purple arrow is $e_- \cdot x$ (only $b$ survives). Together they reconstruct $x$.

```{ojs}
mutable projPt = ({u: 2.0, v: 0.8})
```

```{ojs}
viz3_2 = {
  const D = 4;
  let cur = {u: projPt.u, v: projPt.v};
  const barH = 140, barW = 50, maxV = D;

  // ── Static plot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const plot = Plot.plot({
    width: 360, height: 360, grid: true, aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, D], label: "u"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "v"},
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}), Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ccc"}),
      Plot.line([[-D,-D],[D,D]], {stroke: COL.ePlus, strokeWidth: 1, opacity: 0.3}),
      Plot.line([[-D,D],[D,-D]], {stroke: COL.eMinus, strokeWidth: 1, opacity: 0.3}),
    ]
  });

  const {xScale, yScale} = plotScales(plot);
  const svg = d3.select(plot);
  const ox = xScale(0), oy = yScale(0);

  // ── Arrowhead markers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const defs = svg.append("defs");
  const addMarker = (id, color) => {
    defs.append("marker").attr("id", id)
      .attr("viewBox", "0 0 10 10").attr("refX", 8).attr("refY", 5)
      .attr("markerWidth", 6).attr("markerHeight", 6)
      .attr("orient", "auto-start-reverse")
      .append("path").attr("d", "M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z").attr("fill", color);
  };
  addMarker("arr32-eplus", COL.ePlus);
  addMarker("arr32-eminus", COL.eMinus);
  addMarker("arr32-main", "#111");

  // ── D3 overlay ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const g = svg.append("g");

  const arrowPlus = g.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.ePlus).attr("stroke-width", 3)
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr32-eplus)");
  const arrowMinus = g.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.eMinus).attr("stroke-width", 3)
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr32-eminus)");
  const dashPlus = g.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.eMinus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "4").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const dashMinus = g.append("line").attr("stroke", COL.ePlus).attr("stroke-dasharray", "4").attr("opacity", 0.5);
  const arrowMain = g.append("line").attr("stroke", "#111").attr("stroke-width", 2)
    .attr("marker-end", "url(#arr32-main)");

  const dot = g.append("circle").attr("r", 6)
    .attr("fill", "#111").attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 1.5)
    .style("cursor", "grab");
  const lblX = g.append("text").attr("font-weight", "bold").attr("font-size", 13);
  const lblPlus = g.append("text").attr("fill", COL.ePlus).attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .attr("font-size", 12).attr("text-anchor", "end");
  const lblMinus = g.append("text").attr("fill", COL.eMinus).attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .attr("font-size", 12);

  // ── Bars (persistent DOM) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
  const mid = barH / 2;
  function makeBar(color, label) {
    const div = document.createElement("div");
    div.style.cssText = "text-align:center;width:70px";
    div.innerHTML = `<svg width="70" height="${barH}">
      <line x1="0" y1="${mid}" x2="70" y2="${mid}" stroke="#ccc"/>
      <rect class="fill" x="15" y="${mid}" width="${barW}" height="1"
            fill="${color}" opacity="0.7" rx="3"/>
      <text class="val" x="40" y="${mid}" text-anchor="middle"
            font-size="11" font-weight="600" fill="${color}"></text>
    </svg>
    <div style="font-size:0.8em;color:#6b7280">${label}</div>`;
    return div;
  }

  function setBar(barDiv, val) {
    const scale = (barH - 20) / (2 * maxV);
    const h = Math.abs(val) * scale;
    const y = val >= 0 ? mid - h : mid;
    const fill = barDiv.querySelector(".fill");
    const txt  = barDiv.querySelector(".val");
    fill.setAttribute("y", y);
    fill.setAttribute("height", Math.max(h, 1));
    txt.setAttribute("y", val >= 0 ? y - 3 : y + h + 12);
    txt.textContent = val.toFixed(1);
  }

  const barA = makeBar(COL.ePlus, "a (e₊)");
  const barB = makeBar(COL.eMinus, "b (e₋)");

  // Info text
  const infoDiv = document.createElement("div");
  infoDiv.style.cssText = "margin-top:12px;font-size:0.85em;text-align:center;color:#6b7280";

  // ── Update ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  function update() {
    const {a, b} = toAB(cur.u, cur.v);
    const projP = {u: a/2, v: a/2};
    const projM = {u: b/2, v: -b/2};

    arrowPlus.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(projP.u)).attr("y2", yScale(projP.v));
    arrowMinus.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(projM.u)).attr("y2", yScale(projM.v));
    dashPlus.attr("x1", xScale(projP.u)).attr("y1", yScale(projP.v))
      .attr("x2", xScale(cur.u)).attr("y2", yScale(cur.v));
    dashMinus.attr("x1", xScale(projM.u)).attr("y1", yScale(projM.v))
      .attr("x2", xScale(cur.u)).attr("y2", yScale(cur.v));
    arrowMain.attr("x1", ox).attr("y1", oy)
      .attr("x2", xScale(cur.u)).attr("y2", yScale(cur.v));

    dot.attr("cx", xScale(cur.u)).attr("cy", yScale(cur.v));
    lblX.attr("x", xScale(cur.u) + 10).attr("y", yScale(cur.v) - 10).text("x");
    lblPlus.attr("x", xScale(projP.u) - 14).attr("y", yScale(projP.v) - 8).text("e₊·x");
    lblMinus.attr("x", xScale(projM.u) + 14).attr("y", yScale(projM.v) + 8).text("e₋·x");

    setBar(barA, a);
    setBar(barB, b);

    infoDiv.innerHTML = `<div><span style="color:${COL.ePlus}">e₊ · x</span> keeps only <span style="color:${COL.ePlus}">a = ${a.toFixed(1)}</span></div>
      <div><span style="color:${COL.eMinus}">e₋ · x</span> keeps only <span style="color:${COL.eMinus}">b = ${b.toFixed(1)}</span></div>
      <div style="margin-top:4px;font-weight:600">e₊·x + e₋·x = x  ✓</div>`;
  }

  // ── Drag ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  dot.call(d3.drag()
    .on("start", function() { d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grabbing"); })
    .on("drag", function(event) {
      const [px, py] = d3.pointer(event, plot);
      cur = {u: snap(xScale.invert(px), -D, D), v: snap(yScale.invert(py), -D, D)};
      update();
    })
    .on("end", function() {
      d3.select(this).style("cursor", "grab");
      mutable projPt = {...cur};
    })
  );

  update();

  // ── Assemble ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  const barsRow = document.createElement("div");
  barsRow.style.cssText = "display:flex;gap:8px;align-items:end";
  barsRow.append(barA, barB);

  const sideDiv = document.createElement("div");
  const sideTitle = document.createElement("div");
  sideTitle.style.cssText = "font-size:0.85em;font-weight:600;margin-bottom:8px;text-align:center";
  sideTitle.textContent = "Channel values";
  sideDiv.append(sideTitle, barsRow, infoDiv);

  const out = document.createElement("div");
  out.style.cssText = "display:flex;gap:16px;align-items:center;flex-wrap:wrap;justify-content:center";
  out.append(plot, sideDiv);
  return out;
}
```

:::

## 7. Pushing the original experiment: what if $t^n=1$?

At that point it was irresistible to ask for the next step. If adjoining $t$ with $t^2=1$ produces a two-point function algebra, what happens if one adjoins a formal symbol satisfying

$$
t^n=1?
$$

The resulting algebra is

$$
A_n = F[t]/(t^n-1).
$$

Before even factoring the polynomial, one structural feature jumps out. The residue classes

$$
1,t,t^2,\dots,t^{n-1}
$$

multiply according to the rule

$$
t^a t^b = t^{a+b \, \mod n}.
$$

So these powers of $t$ form a cyclic multiplicative group of order $n$, isomorphic to the additive group $(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z,+)$. Once linear combinations are allowed, the experiment has quietly moved from a cyclic group to its group algebra. The basis

$$
1,t,t^2,\dots,t^{n-1}
$$

is therefore not just a convenient spanning set. It is the natural basis coming from the group elements themselves.

At first I half-expected some neat $n$-fold real splitting. The real answer depends entirely on how the polynomial $t^n-1$ factors over the base field.

### Over $\mathbb C$

Over the complex numbers,

$$
t^n-1=\prod_{k=0}^{n-1}(t-\omega^k),
\qquad
\omega=e^{2\pi i/n},
$$

and the roots are all distinct. So one gets

$$
\mathbb C[t]/(t^n-1)\cong \mathbb C^n.
$$

Concretely,

$$
f(t) \longmapsto \bigl(f(1),f(\omega),f(\omega^2),\dots,f(\omega^{n-1})\bigr).
$$

So over $\mathbb C$, the quotient really is the algebra of functions on the finite set of $n$th roots of unity.

This was the point where something I had been feeling only vaguely snapped into focus. The roots of unity are not, by themselves, “the basis” of $\mathbb C^n$. Rather, they index one natural coordinate system: evaluation at the $n$ points $1,\omega,\dots,\omega^{n-1}$. 

### Over $\mathbb R$

Over the reals, the picture is only partly split. Real roots contribute one-dimensional real factors, while nonreal roots occur in complex-conjugate pairs and produce quadratic irreducible factors. As real algebras this gives

- if $n$ is odd,
  $$
  \mathbb R[t]/(t^n-1)\cong \mathbb R\times \mathbb C^{(n-1)/2},
  $$
- if $n$ is even,
  $$
  \mathbb R[t]/(t^n-1)\cong \mathbb R\times \mathbb R\times \mathbb C^{(n-2)/2}.
  $$

So the perfectly real splitting into independent one-dimensional axes is a special luxury of the case $n=2$.

## 8. The higher idempotents and the Fourier change of basis

Over $\mathbb C[t]/(t^n-1)$ there is a direct analogue of the pair $e_+,e_-$.

Let $\omega=e^{2\pi i/n}$ and define, for $j=0,1,\dots,n-1$,

$$
e_j=\frac1n\sum_{m=0}^{n-1} \omega^{-jm} t^m.
$$

These are the Fourier idempotents. They satisfy

$$
e_j^2=e_j,
\qquad
e_je_k=0 \text{ for } j\neq k,
\qquad
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1} e_j=1.
$$

So they form a complete set of orthogonal idempotents.

Under the isomorphism with $\mathbb C^n$, each $e_j$ becomes the function that is $1$ at $\omega^j$ and $0$ at all the other roots. In other words, each $e_j$ is just a singleton indicator in disguise. Once again the algebra has turned into a finite function algebra, and once again the distinguished basis is the atomic one.

But now there is an extra layer of structure. The change of basis is explicit in both directions:

$$
e_j=\frac1n\sum_{m=0}^{n-1} \omega^{-jm} t^m,
$$

and conversely,

$$
t^m=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\omega^{jm} e_j.
$$

So if $u=(u_0,\dots,u_{n-1})^\top$ is the coordinate vector in the power basis $\{1,t,\dots,t^{n-1}\}$ and $a=(a_0,\dots,a_{n-1})^\top$ is the coordinate vector in the idempotent basis $\{e_0,\dots,e_{n-1}\}$, then

$$
u = Wa,
\qquad
a=\frac1n\overline{W}\,u,
$$

where

$$
W_{mj}=\omega^{mj}.
$$

That is exactly the discrete Fourier transform matrix and its inverse, up to the usual normalization. If one rescales by $1/\sqrt n$, one gets the standard unitary DFT matrix.

This was one of my favorite moments in the whole exploration. I had not just rediscovered a family of orthogonal idempotents. I had backed into the DFT itself: it is the linear change of coordinates between the basis coming from the cyclic group elements and the basis coming from point masses on the corresponding finite function algebra.

There is also a clean group-theoretic way to say the same thing. The powers $t^m$ are indexed by the cyclic group $C_n\cong \mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z$. The functions

$$
\chi_m(k)=\omega^{mk}
$$

are its characters. The formulas above are precisely the Fourier transform and Fourier inversion for that finite abelian group.

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: roots of unity and their indicator idempotents.** Adjust $n$ to see the $n$th roots of unity on the complex unit circle (left). Each root $\omega^j$ indexes one idempotent $e_j$ — a function that evaluates to $1$ at that root and $0$ everywhere else (right). Click a root to highlight its idempotent. For $n = 2$, the two roots are $1$ and $-1$, and the idempotents are exactly $e_+$ and $e_-$ from our original discovery.

```{ojs}
viewof nRoots = Inputs.range([2, 128], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "n  (order of  tⁿ = 1)"})
mutable selectedJ = 0
```

```{ojs}
// Slider synced to mutable — click on a root or bar to update
viz5_slider = {
  const max = nRoots - 1;
  const clamped = Math.min(selectedJ, max);
  const inp = Inputs.range([0, max], {value: clamped, step: 1, label: "Highlight idempotent  eⱼ"});
  inp.addEventListener("input", () => { mutable selectedJ = +inp.value; });
  return inp;
}
```

```{ojs}
viz5_1 = {
  const n = nRoots;
  const j = Math.min(selectedJ, n - 1);  // clamp
  const omega = (k) => ({re: Math.cos(2*Math.PI*k/n), im: Math.sin(2*Math.PI*k/n)});

  // Phase → color function (same as DFT grid)
  function phaseColor(angle) {
    const cosA = Math.cos(angle);
    const sinA = Math.sin(angle);
    const hueT = (1 + cosA) / 2;
    const hue = hueT * 180;
    const c = d3.hsl(hue, 0.75, 0.55);
    const blend = Math.abs(sinA);
    if (sinA >= 0) {
      c.l *= (1 - blend * 0.5);
      c.s += (1 - c.s) * blend;
    } else {
      c.l += (1 - c.l) * blend * 0.8;
      c.s *= (1 - blend * 0.7);
    }
    return c.formatHex();
  }

  // Phase color blended with white at the same opacity used by ring/grid
  function phaseColorBlended(angle) {
    const raw = d3.hsl(phaseColor(angle));
    const alpha = 0.45;
    raw.l = raw.l * alpha + 1.0 * (1 - alpha);  // blend lightness toward white
    raw.s = raw.s * alpha;                        // reduce saturation proportionally
    return raw.formatHex();
  }

  // ── Left: roots on unit circle ────────────────────────────────────────
  // Phase-colored ring segments
  const nSegs = 360;
  const ringData = Array.from({length: nSegs}, (_, i) => {
    const a0 = i * 2 * Math.PI / nSegs;
    const a1 = (i + 1) * 2 * Math.PI / nSegs;
    const amid = (a0 + a1) / 2;
    return {x1: Math.cos(a0) * 0.85, y1: Math.sin(a0) * 0.85,
            x2: Math.cos(a1) * 0.85, y2: Math.sin(a1) * 0.85,
            color: phaseColor(amid)};
  });

  const circleData = Array.from({length: 201}, (_, i) => {
    const t = i * 2 * Math.PI / 200;
    return {x: Math.cos(t), y: Math.sin(t)};
  });

  const roots = Array.from({length: n}, (_, k) => {
    const w = omega(k);
    return {x: w.re, y: w.im, k, label: k === 0 ? "1" : k === 1 ? "ω" : `ω${superscript(k)}`};
  });

  // Build left plot as SVG to support thick colored ring
  const LW = 300, LH = 300;
  const lsvg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", LW).attr("height", LH)
    .attr("viewBox", "-1.6 -1.6 3.2 3.2")
    .style("overflow", "visible");

  // Axes
  lsvg.append("line").attr("x1",-1.5).attr("x2",1.5).attr("y1",0).attr("y2",0).attr("stroke","#ddd").attr("stroke-width",0.01);
  lsvg.append("line").attr("x1",0).attr("x2",0).attr("y1",-1.5).attr("y2",1.5).attr("stroke","#ddd").attr("stroke-width",0.01);

  // Colored ring segments
  const ringWidth = 0.18;
  for (const seg of ringData) {
    const a0 = Math.atan2(seg.y1, seg.x1);
    const a1 = Math.atan2(seg.y2, seg.x2);
    const r1 = 1 - ringWidth / 2, r2 = 1 + ringWidth / 2;
    const x1o = r2 * Math.cos(a0), y1o = -r2 * Math.sin(a0);
    const x2o = r2 * Math.cos(a1), y2o = -r2 * Math.sin(a1);
    const x1i = r1 * Math.cos(a1), y1i = -r1 * Math.sin(a1);
    const x2i = r1 * Math.cos(a0), y2i = -r1 * Math.sin(a0);
    lsvg.append("path")
      .attr("d", `M${x1o},${y1o} A${r2},${r2} 0 0,0 ${x2o},${y2o} L${x1i},${y1i} A${r1},${r1} 0 0,1 ${x2i},${y2i} Z`)
      .attr("fill", seg.color)
      .attr("opacity", 0.45)
      .attr("stroke", "none");
  }

  // Clickable arc sectors — one per root, covering the full ring width
  const halfArc = Math.PI / n;  // half the angular distance between adjacent roots
  const r1hit = 1 - ringWidth / 2, r2hit = 1 + ringWidth / 2;
  for (let k = 0; k < n; k++) {
    const centerAngle = 2 * Math.PI * k / n;
    const a0 = centerAngle - halfArc, a1 = centerAngle + halfArc;
    const x1o = r2hit * Math.cos(a0), y1o = -r2hit * Math.sin(a0);
    const x2o = r2hit * Math.cos(a1), y2o = -r2hit * Math.sin(a1);
    const x1i = r1hit * Math.cos(a1), y1i = -r1hit * Math.sin(a1);
    const x2i = r1hit * Math.cos(a0), y2i = -r1hit * Math.sin(a0);
    const largeArc = (a1 - a0) > Math.PI ? 1 : 0;
    lsvg.append("path")
      .attr("d", `M${x1o},${y1o} A${r2hit},${r2hit} 0 ${largeArc},0 ${x2o},${y2o} L${x1i},${y1i} A${r1hit},${r1hit} 0 ${largeArc},1 ${x2i},${y2i} Z`)
      .attr("fill", "transparent")
      .style("cursor", "pointer")
      .on("click", () => { mutable selectedJ = k; });
  }

  // Root dots — use phase color matching ring/grid, clickable
  for (const r of roots) {
    const rootAngle = 2 * Math.PI * r.k / n;
    const dot = lsvg.append("circle")
      .attr("cx", r.x).attr("cy", -r.y)
      .style("cursor", "pointer")
      .on("click", () => { mutable selectedJ = r.k; });
    if (r.k === j) {
      dot.attr("r", 0.07)
        .attr("fill", phaseColorBlended(rootAngle))
        .attr("stroke", "white").attr("stroke-width", 0.015);
    } else {
      dot.attr("r", 0.04)
        .attr("fill", "none")
        .attr("stroke", "#999").attr("stroke-width", 0.012);
    }
  }

  // Root labels — clickable
  for (const r of roots) {
    const rootAngle = 2 * Math.PI * r.k / n;
    lsvg.append("text")
      .attr("x", r.x + (r.x >= 0 ? 0.1 : -0.1))
      .attr("y", -r.y + (r.y >= 0 ? -0.08 : 0.12))
      .attr("text-anchor", r.x >= 0 ? "start" : "end")
      .attr("font-size", 0.09)
      .attr("font-weight", r.k === j ? "bold" : "normal")
      .attr("fill", r.k === j ? phaseColorBlended(rootAngle) : "#999")
      .style("cursor", "pointer")
      .on("click", () => { mutable selectedJ = r.k; })
      .text(r.label);
  }

  const leftPlot = lsvg.node();

  // ── Right: indicator bar chart for e_j ────────────────────────────────
  const barData = Array.from({length: n}, (_, k) => ({
    k,
    val: k === j ? 1 : 0,
    label: k === 0 ? "1" : k === 1 ? "ω" : `ω${superscript(k)}`
  }));

  const rightPlot = Plot.plot({
    width: 300, height: 280,
    x: {domain: barData.map(d => d.label), label: "Evaluation point", padding: 0.3},
    y: {domain: [-0.2, 1.3], label: "Value", grid: true},
    marks: [
      Plot.ruleY([0]),
      Plot.barY(barData, {x: "label", y: "val",
        fill: d => d.k === j ? phaseColorBlended(2 * Math.PI * d.k / n) : "#e5e7eb",
        stroke: d => d.k === j ? phaseColorBlended(2 * Math.PI * d.k / n) : "#ccc",
      }),
      Plot.text(barData.filter(d => d.val > 0), {x: "label", y: "val",
        text: ["1"], dy: -8, fontWeight: "bold", fontSize: 13,
        fill: phaseColorBlended(2 * Math.PI * j / n)
      }),
    ]
  });

  // Make bar chart x-axis ticks and bars clickable
  const rp = d3.select(rightPlot);
  rp.selectAll("[aria-label='x-axis'] .tick text").each(function(d, i) {
    d3.select(this).style("cursor", "pointer").on("click", () => { mutable selectedJ = i; });
  });
  rp.selectAll("rect").each(function(d, i) {
    d3.select(this).style("cursor", "pointer").on("click", () => { mutable selectedJ = i; });
  });

  return html`<div class="dual-panel">
    <div>
      <div class="viz-label">Roots of unity (n = ${n})</div>
      ${leftPlot}
    </div>
    <div>
      <div class="viz-label">${tex`e_{${j}}`}: indicator at ${tex`\omega^{${j}}`}</div>
      ${rightPlot}
      <div style="font-size:0.8em;color:#6b7280;text-align:center;margin-top:4px">
        ${tex`e_{${j}} = \tfrac{1}{${n}}\sum_{m=0}^{${n-1}} \omega^{-${j}m}\,t^m`}
      </div>
    </div>
  </div>`;
}

// Helper for Unicode superscripts
superscript = (n) => String(n).split('').map(d => '⁰¹²³⁴⁵⁶⁷⁸⁹'[+d]).join('')
```

:::

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: the DFT matrix as a grid of phasors.** Each cell shows the complex number $\omega^{mj}$ as a small arrow. Row $m$ is the power-basis index; column $j$ is the idempotent index. This matrix IS the change-of-basis between $\{1, t, \dots, t^{n-1}\}$ and $\{e_0, \dots, e_{n-1}\}$. For $n = 2$, it's just $\begin{pmatrix}1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1\end{pmatrix}$ — the very same matrix we saw earlier.

```{ojs}
viz5_2 = {
  const n = nRoots;
  const maxWidth = width - 30;
  const fixedCell = 60;
  const fixedPad = 40;
  const cellSize = Math.min(fixedCell, (maxWidth - fixedPad) / n);
  const pad = fixedPad * (cellSize / fixedCell);
  const labelPad = 18;
  const W = n * cellSize + pad, H = n * cellSize + pad / 2 + labelPad;
  const arrowLen = cellSize * 0.35;

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("width", W + 30).attr("height", H + 10);

  // Column headers (j)
  const headerFontSize = Math.max(7, Math.min(11, cellSize * 0.6));
  for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    svg.append("text")
      .attr("x", pad + j * cellSize + cellSize / 2)
      .attr("y", labelPad - 4)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle")
      .attr("font-size", headerFontSize)
      .attr("fill", "#6b7280")
      .text(`e${superscript(j)}`);
  }

  // Row headers (m)
  for (let m = 0; m < n; m++) {
    svg.append("text")
      .attr("x", pad - 8)
      .attr("y", labelPad + m * cellSize + cellSize / 2 + 4)
      .attr("text-anchor", "end")
      .attr("font-size", headerFontSize)
      .attr("fill", "#6b7280")
      .text(m === 0 ? "1" : m === 1 ? "t" : `t${superscript(m)}`);
  }

  for (let m = 0; m < n; m++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      const angle = 2 * Math.PI * m * j / n;
      const cx = pad + j * cellSize + cellSize / 2;
      const cy = labelPad + m * cellSize + cellSize / 2;

      // Background cell — circular phase color map:
      //   cos(angle) → hue: continuous rotation, red(0°) at +1, blue(240°) at −1
      //   sin(angle) → lightness: dark+vivid at +i, light+pale at −i
      const cosA = Math.cos(angle);
      const sinA = Math.sin(angle);
      // Smooth hue: 180° (cyan) at +1, rotate through 90° to 0° (red) at −1
      const hueT = (1 + cosA) / 2;          // 1 at +1, 0 at −1
      const hue = hueT * 180;               // 180° → 0° (cyan → red, complementary)
      const baseSat = 0.75;
      const baseLight = 0.55;
      const c = d3.hsl(hue, baseSat, baseLight);
      const blend = Math.abs(sinA);
      if (sinA >= 0) {
        // Top half: darken + increase saturation toward vivid dark at +i
        c.l *= (1 - blend * 0.5);
        c.s += (1 - c.s) * blend;
      } else {
        // Bottom half: lighten + desaturate toward pale/white at −i
        c.l += (1 - c.l) * blend * 0.8;
        c.s *= (1 - blend * 0.7);
      }
      svg.append("rect")
        .attr("x", cx - cellSize / 2 + 1)
        .attr("y", cy - cellSize / 2 + 1)
        .attr("width", cellSize - cellSize/fixedCell)
        .attr("height", cellSize - cellSize/fixedCell)
        .attr("fill", c.formatHex())
        .attr("opacity", 0.45)
        .attr("rx", 3);

      // Arrow (phasor)
      const dx = arrowLen * Math.cos(angle);
      const dy = -arrowLen * Math.sin(angle);  // SVG y is flipped
      svg.append("line")
        .attr("x1", cx).attr("y1", cy)
        .attr("x2", cx + dx).attr("y2", cy + dy)
        .attr("stroke", "#374151")
        .attr("stroke-width", Math.max(0.5, cellSize * 0.05))
        .attr("marker-end", "url(#arrow5_2)");

      // Dot at center
      svg.append("circle")
        .attr("cx", cx).attr("cy", cy).attr("r", Math.max(1, cellSize * 0.033)).attr("fill", "#9ca3af");
    }
  }

  // Arrow marker
  const markerSize = Math.min(5, Math.max(3, cellSize));
  svg.append("defs").append("marker")
    .attr("id", "arrow5_2")
    .attr("viewBox", "0 0 10 10")
    .attr("refX", 7.5).attr("refY", 5)
    .attr("markerWidth", markerSize).attr("markerHeight", markerSize)
    .attr("orient", "auto-start-reverse")
    .append("path").attr("d", "M 0 2 L 10 5 L 0 8 z").attr("fill", "#374151");

  return html`<div style="text-align:center">
    <div style="font-size:0.85em;color:#6b7280;margin-bottom:4px">DFT matrix ${tex`W_{mj} = \omega^{mj}`} for n = ${n}</div>
    ${svg.node()}
    ${n === 2 ? html`<div style="font-size:0.85em;color:#6b7280;margin-top:8px">
      ${tex`W = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}`} — exactly the ${tex`e_+, e_-`} change of basis!
    </div>` : html``}
  </div>`;
}
```

:::

::: {.viz-wrap}

**Explore: constructive and destructive interference.** This shows the term-by-term vector addition that builds the idempotent $e_j$ evaluated at $\omega^k$. Each arrow is one term $\frac{1}{n}\omega^{(k-j)m}$. When $j = k$, all arrows point right and **add up to 1** (constructive interference). When $j \neq k$, the arrows fan into a closed polygon and **cancel to exactly 0** (destructive interference). This is *why* $e_j$ is an indicator function.

```{ojs}
viewof interParams = Inputs.form({
  j_int: Inputs.range([0, 7], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "Target idempotent  j"}),
  k_int: Inputs.range([0, 7], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "Evaluation point  k"}),
})
```

```{ojs}
viz5_3 = {
  const n = nRoots;
  const j = Math.min(interParams.j_int, n - 1);
  const k = Math.min(interParams.k_int, n - 1);

  // Vectors: (1/n) * ω^{(k-j)m} for m = 0..n-1
  const vectors = Array.from({length: n}, (_, m) => {
    const angle = (k - j) * m * 2 * Math.PI / n;
    return {dx: Math.cos(angle) / n, dy: Math.sin(angle) / n, m};
  });

  // Cumulative endpoints
  const cumul = [];
  let cx = 0, cy = 0;
  for (const v of vectors) {
    const start = {x: cx, y: cy};
    cx += v.dx; cy += v.dy;
    cumul.push({start, end: {x: cx, y: cy}, m: v.m});
  }
  const sumX = cx, sumY = cy;

  // Build marks
  const colors = d3.schemeTableau10;
  const D = 0.7;

  const marks = [
    Plot.ruleY([0], {stroke: "#ddd"}), Plot.ruleX([0], {stroke: "#ddd"}),
  ];

  // Individual vectors
  for (const seg of cumul) {
    marks.push(Plot.arrow([seg], {
      x1: d => d.start.x, y1: d => d.start.y,
      x2: d => d.end.x, y2: d => d.end.y,
      stroke: colors[seg.m % 10], strokeWidth: 2.5
    }));
    marks.push(Plot.dot([seg.end], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: colors[seg.m % 10], r: 3}));
  }

  // Closing line back to origin (for j ≠ k, shows the polygon)
  if (Math.abs(sumX) + Math.abs(sumY) < 0.01 && j !== k) {
    marks.push(Plot.line([[cumul[cumul.length-1].end.x, cumul[cumul.length-1].end.y], [0, 0]], {
      stroke: "#ef4444", strokeDasharray: "4", strokeWidth: 1.5}));
  }

  // Sum indicator
  marks.push(
    Plot.dot([{x: 1, y: 0}], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#ef4444", r: 5, symbol: "diamond"}),
    Plot.text([{x: 1, y: 0}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: ["1"], dx: 10, fontSize: 12, fill: "#ef4444", fontWeight: "bold"}),
    Plot.dot([{x: sumX, y: sumY}], {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "#111", r: 5, stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 1.5}),
    Plot.text([{x: sumX, y: sumY}], {x: "x", y: "y",
      text: [`Σ = ${sumX.toFixed(3)} + ${sumY.toFixed(3)}i`],
      dx: 10, dy: -10, fontSize: 11, fontWeight: 600}),
  );

  const plot = Plot.plot({
    width: 450, height: 450,
    aspectRatio: 1,
    x: {domain: [-D, 1.3], label: "Real"},
    y: {domain: [-D, D], label: "Imaginary"},
    marks
  });

  const outcome = Math.abs(sumX - 1) + Math.abs(sumY) < 0.01 ? "= 1  ✓  (constructive)" :
                   Math.abs(sumX) + Math.abs(sumY) < 0.01 ? "= 0  ✓  (destructive)" :
                   `≈ ${sumX.toFixed(3)}`;

  return html`<div style="text-align:center">
    ${plot}
    <div style="margin-top:8px;font-size:1em">
      ${tex`e_{${j}}(\omega^{${k}})`} <strong>${outcome}</strong>
      ${j === k
        ? html`<div style="color:${COL.ePlus};font-size:0.88em;margin-top:4px">All arrows point right → perfect alignment</div>`
        : html`<div style="color:${COL.hyper};font-size:0.88em;margin-top:4px">Arrows fan into a closed polygon → perfect cancellation</div>`
      }
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

:::

## 9. One last extension: infinite sets

The same picture survives for an arbitrary, potentially infinite, set $S$, but now one has to distinguish between a pointwise decomposition and a genuine Hamel-basis expansion. We now ignore the original motivation for $t^n = 1$ and work directly with the indicators.

Consider the algebra

$$
F^S
$$

of all functions $f:S\to F$.

The singleton indicators still exist:

$$
\delta_s := \mathbf 1_{\{s\}}, \qquad s\in S.
$$

They still satisfy

$$
\delta_s^2=\delta_s,
\qquad
\delta_s\delta_t=0 \text{ for } s\neq t.
$$

And every function still has the pointwise decomposition

$$
f(u)=\sum_{s\in S} f(s)\,\delta_s(u)
\qquad (u\in S),
$$

because at a given point $u$, exactly one term survives: the one with $s=u$.

But if $S$ is infinite, that does **not** make the family $\{\delta_s\}_{s\in S}$ a Hamel basis of all of $F^S$, because Hamel bases allow only finite linear combinations. What we really have is an atomic product decomposition

$$
F^S \cong \prod_{s\in S} F,
$$

and the indicator family records those coordinates pointwise.

For the subspace of finitely supported functions,

$$
F^{(S)},
$$

the story does become literally basis-theoretic: the singleton indicators are a genuine basis.

If $|S|=|\mathbb R| = |\mathbb C| = 2^{\aleph_0}$, one may also choose a bijection

$$
T:\mathbb R \to S
$$

and relabel the singleton indicators by real or complex numbers:

$$
\delta_x := \mathbf 1_{\{T(x)\}}, \qquad x\in\mathbb R \text{ or } \mathbb C.
$$

That changes only the labels, not the structure. At that stage the role of $\mathbb R$ is purely as an index set; no topology, order, or measure has entered yet.

## 10. Under square summability, an honest orthonormal basis

The final refinement appears when one passes from all functions on $S$ to square-summable ones. With counting measure, this gives the Hilbert space

$$
\ell^2(S).
$$

Now the singleton indicators really do form an orthonormal basis:

$$
\langle \delta_s,\delta_t\rangle = \delta_{s,t},
$$

with $\delta_{s,t}$ the usual Kronecker delta. And every $f\in \ell^2(S)$ has the expansion

$$
f = \sum_{s\in S} f(s) \, \delta_s
$$

with convergence in the $\ell^2$ norm.

So in this setting the analogy becomes exact. The same family that serves as multiplicative atoms in the algebra $F^S$ also serves as orthonormal coordinates in the Hilbert space $\ell^2(S)$.

I liked that as an ending to the exploration. The basis first appeared as an algebraic curiosity inside the split-complex numbers, and only later revealed itself as something that also belongs naturally to Hilbert-space geometry.

## 11. Looking back

Seen from the end, the thread is surprisingly clean. A single sign flip produced zero divisors; the zero divisors suggested the factors $1+t$ and $1-t$; those suggested idempotents; the idempotents diagonalized multiplication; diagonalized multiplication exposed a direct product; and the direct product turned out to be the function algebra on a two-point set. From there the finite-set picture, the cyclic-group generalization, and the Fourier change of basis follow almost inevitably. The infinite case then appears as a final extension, with the same indicator family surviving pointwise and becoming a genuine orthonormal basis once one passes to $\ell^2(S)$.

So the deeper principle hiding inside the experiment seems to be this:

> Whenever an algebra splits into orthogonal idempotent pieces, it begins to behave like a function algebra on a set of mutually independent points.

That is why the basis felt so natural. It was not merely convenient. It was the algebraic analogue of decomposing a space into disjoint atoms.

And that, for me, is one of the pleasures of returning to old mathematics seriously: not just remembering the answer, but watching the answer reveal the structure that was already waiting inside the first small question.

None of this is new mathematics. But starting from the tiny prompt “what if $t^2=1$?” and following the consequences by hand made the web of ideas feel unusually vivid to me. That was reason enough to keep going.
